quoteLast: , we descried the land. ... It appeared a mean high land, as we after found it, being an island of some six miles in compass, but I hope the most fortunate ever yet discovered....
title: A Source Book in American History to 1787
author: West, Willis M. (Willis Mason), 1857-1931 [Editor]

quoteLast: of the 31st, the ice loosened close along the ice-foot towards Lincoln Bay, but before our lines could be cast off it had closed in again. From here I sent five Eskimos across overland after the skins of some musk-oxen which had been killed earlier in the season.
title:
Nearest the Pole
a narrative of the polar expedition of the Peary Arctic Club in the S.S. Roosevelt, 1905-1906
author: Peary, Robert E. (Robert Edwin), 1856-1920; Operti, Albert, 1852-1927 [Illustrator]

quoteLast: , I should fancy, the noisy minstrels appeared in the court, headed by Fenn with a lantern, and knocking together as they came. The visitors clambered noisily into the gig, one of them shook the reins, and they were snatched out of sight and hearing with a suddenness that partook of the nature of prodigy.
title: The Works of Robert Louis Stevenson - Swanston Edition, Vol. 20
author: Stevenson, Robert Louis, 1850-1894; Quiller-Couch, Arthur, 1863-1944 [Contributor]

quoteLast: . The Abbot then explained the chapter which had been sung, dwelt on the obligations incumbent on his hearers, by their profession, to observe the teaching which St. Benedict inculcated by his Rule; then called for the public self-accusations of breaches of monastic discipline (external faults only), and imposed penances commensurate with each transgression.
title:
Mellifont Abbey, Co. Louth
Its Ruins and Associations, a Guide and Popular History
author: Anonymous

quoteFirst: It seems a pity to spoil such finely laid out grounds, but such is war. The whole Division got ready to move
quoteLast: but as the enemy remained quiet we did. There's no picket firing to-night. I'm so tired and lousy I do wish we could stay somewhere long enough to wash and boil our underclothing.
title: Personal Recollections and Civil War Diary, 1864
author: Abbott, Lemuel Abijah, 1842-1911

quoteFirst: In 1802, the mails between Edinburgh and Glasgow were still conveyed by men travelling on horseback--the route taken being by way of Falkirk--the hour of despatch from Glasgow being 9 P.M., and the hour of arrival in Edinburgh
quoteLast: or 7 A.M. The riders of this mail seem to have had sections of the road apportioned to them--one rider covering the road from Glasgow to Falkirk, the other taking the stage from Falkirk to Edinburgh. On the morning of the 1st of August in that year, the rider for the east stage--named William Wilson--received the Glasgow mail-bag entire and duly sealed at Falkirk, and thereafter set out towards Edinburgh.
title: The Royal Mail: Its Curiosities and Romance
author: Hyde, James Wilson, 1841-1918

quoteFirst: Apart from giving pin-points of the far distant enemy there was nothing to be done except talk to all and sundry and try and get news. Some French machine-gunner officers appeared who told us that the entire French army was moving by forced marches to assist in stopping the advance and were due to arrive
title: The Grey Wave
author: Gibbs, A. Hamilton (Arthur Hamilton), 1888-1964; Gibbs, Philip, 1877-1962 [Author of introduction, etc.]

quoteLast: , the Minister drove off in his carriage, accompanied by Count Bismarck-Bohlen, and followed on horseback by Herr Abeken, Herr von Keudell, and Count Hatzfeldt. The rest of us remained behind, where we had plenty of work on hand, and could make ourselves useful in other ways.
title: Bismarck
author: Busch, Moritz, 1821-1899

quoteFirst: Sir Edward and his staff and the British correspondents left next morning early,
quoteLast: No untoward incidents occurred at the time of their departure which was, of course, unknown to the populace of Berlin.
title: My Four Years in Germany
author: Gerard, James W. (James Watson), 1867-1951

quoteFirst: Towards midnight, however, a thick fog set in, and we were obliged to anchor till morning. The fog cleared away
quoteLast: , and we found ourselves lying opposite a small village which seemed to be deep in slumber. Not for long, though.
title: The War Stories of Private Thomas Atkins
author: Milne, James, 1865-1951
